vulnerability.title: Disable SSH Support for .rhosts Files
vulnerability.description: SSH can emulate the behavior of the obsolete rsh command in allowing users to enable insecure access to their accounts via .rhosts files. The default SSH configuration disables support for .rhosts. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for IgnoreRhosts. To explicitly disable support for .rhosts files, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: IgnoreRhosts yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complia

triage_decision.reason: Disabling IgnoreRhosts is a standard SSH hardening step that does not risk lockout or critical service disruption.

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for sshd_disable_rhosts (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sshd_disable_rhosts). Could not verify.

llm_verdict.message:
Below is the step‑by‑step procedure that I would carry out once a stable SSH connection is available.  
The plan follows the guidelines exactly – no chaining, a single command per tool call, and minimal file changes.

---

## 1. Create the configuration fragment if it does not yet exist
```bash
mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d
```

## 2. Ensure the file is readable/writable ONLY by root
```bash
chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

## 3. Inspect the current file (create a “touch” if it’s missing)
```bash
touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

## 4. Remove any existing `IgnoreRhosts` directive (case‑insensitive, leading‑space tolerant)
```bash
sed -i '/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

## 5. Append the required line – add a terminating newline first only if one does not exist
```bash
printf '\nIgnoreRhosts yes\n' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

*(If the file already ends with a newline, this will add the directive without double‑spacing; if it does not, the `printf '\n'` guarantees a clean separator.)*

## 6. Verify the syntax of the new SSH configuration
```bash
sshd -t
```
If this command exits with code 0, the syntax is valid.

## 7. Reload SSH to apply the change
```bash
systemctl reload sshd
```

---

### Why each step is safe

| Step | Rationale |
|------|-----------|
| 1 | `mkdir -p` is harmless – it only creates the directory if needed. |
| 2 | The file is set to `0600` so that only root can write to it; existing read/write permissions remain unchanged for root. |
| 3 | `touch` fixes the “file not found” case and preserves the file if it already exists. |
| 4 | The `sed -i` command deletes *any* previous `IgnoreRhosts` line, ensuring the configuration is idempotent. |
| 5 | `printf` appends the exact directive without duplicating an existing line; the preceding newline prevents unintended concatenation. |
| 6 | `sshd -t` validates no syntax errors – if it fails, I would roll back the change by editing the file manually. |
| 7 | `systemctl reload` applies the config without full restart, protecting any transient SSH connections. |

---

### Expected outcome

After these operations, `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` will contain exactly one line:

```
IgnoreRhosts yes
```

The SSH service will be reloaded with this new setting, and the scanner will observe that the “sshd_disable_rhosts” finding is resolved.

reasoning_messages.content:
Below is the step‑by‑step procedure that I would carry out once a stable SSH connection is available.  
The plan follows the guidelines exactly – no chaining, a single command per tool call, and minimal file changes.

---

## 1. Create the configuration fragment if it does not yet exist
```bash
mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d
```

## 2. Ensure the file is readable/writable ONLY by root
```bash
chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

## 3. Inspect the current file (create a “touch” if it’s missing)
```bash
touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

## 4. Remove any existing `IgnoreRhosts` directive (case‑insensitive, leading‑space tolerant)
```bash
sed -i '/^\s*IgnoreRhosts\s\+/Id' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

## 5. Append the required line – add a terminating newline first only if one does not exist
```bash
printf '\nIgnoreRhosts yes\n' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
```

*(If the file already ends with a newline, this will add the directive without double‑spacing; if it does not, the `printf '\n'` guarantees a clean separator.)*

## 6. Verify the syntax of the new SSH configuration
```bash
sshd -t
```
If this command exits with code 0, the syntax is valid.

## 7. Reload SSH to apply the change
```bash
systemctl reload sshd
```

---

### Why each step is safe

| Step | Rationale |
|------|-----------|
| 1 | `mkdir -p` is harmless – it only creates the directory if needed. |
| 2 | The file is set to `0600` so that only root can write to it; existing read/write permissions remain unchanged for root. |
| 3 | `touch` fixes the “file not found” case and preserves the file if it already exists. |
| 4 | The `sed -i` command deletes *any* previous `IgnoreRhosts` line, ensuring the configuration is idempotent. |
| 5 | `printf` appends the exact directive without duplicating an existing line; the preceding newline prevents unintended concatenation. |
| 6 | `sshd -t` validates no syntax errors – if it fails, I would roll back the change by editing the file manually. |
| 7 | `systemctl reload` applies the config without full restart, protecting any transient SSH connections. |

---

### Expected outcome

After these operations, `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` will contain exactly one line:

```
IgnoreRhosts yes
```

The SSH service will be reloaded with this new setting, and the scanner will observe that the “sshd_disable_rhosts” finding is resolved.
